Media notes: I stood about 2 feet away from a set of three bird feeders.  The number of unique individual chickadees recorded is not known since it was not possible to track the individuals coming to and fro while standing still.  Sounds include their calls, the non-vocal sound of their wingbeats as they took off from the feeders or the nearby tree to their destination, and an occasional plink sound usually associated with the metal hopper feeder.  The other two feeders are tubes.  All three are filled with black oil sunflower seeds.  Ironically, it seemed like they were less comfortable visiting the feeders when I averted my eyes (looked down) than when I looked them straight in the eye.  When my eyes were averted it seemed like they landed then immediately took off without taking a seed. Or they fled from the nearby leafless deciduous tree without coming for a seed.  Background noise includes distant vehicular traffic.  There was no appreciable wind. The one second gap at the beginning removes a segment of handling noise when I flinched when one of the birds flew toward me - probably was going to fly past - but I was not expecting it. None of them tried to fly past me after that, once they realized the object was a human and not some regular stationary thing.

This is the first time I'm calling BCCH south of the BCCH-CACH contact zone. In recent winters, I've heard chickadees that sound more like BCCH than CACH and seen individuals that looked different than the chickadees we have here in the summer, but I have been (and remain) skeptical of our ability to reliably identify BCCH in the field given our proximity to the hybrid zone. If BCCH move south, then so too could hybrids, right? I've been hearing the slow "dee-dee-dee" from chickadees at home recently, but I've yet to get a good look at any. In fact, I hadn't done a deep dive into chickadee ID as I, in my infinite wisdom, assumed that I wouldn't hear and see any well enough to feel comfortable pushing the envelope with a call. I've still been keeping an ear out, and the first chickadee I had today sounded "normal". I was able to see it at a distance and I noticed that it was very gray and drab overall. As Will and I were leaving the marshy area, we heard more chickadees, and their vocalizations were unmistakably distinct from those of the first chickadee. We tracked the bird down and, surprisingly, it was foraging with others just a few feet off the ground. It gave us great looks! Those looks revealed a strikingly patterned bird: vibrant flanks, bright white in the wings giving a sharply contrasting overall appearance. Will expertly captured just about every possible angle of the bird that we watched produce BCCH calls (and songs). I took a close look at these photos and did as much digging as possible to see which boxes need to be checked to be confident this is a black-capped. First, I wanted to explore the question stated earlier: if we're getting an influx of BCCH, shouldn't we also get an influx of hybrids? I'd have assumed the answer to be yes, but Sibley and other sources mention that the BCCH that occasionally push south are northern birds (as in, "far" north; not northern PA). If some sort of pressure was forcing BCCH south out of the area just north of the contact zone, we could expect hybrids to move as well: they'd conceivably be faced with the same food shortage, etc., impacting the BCCH. If the BCCH coming south are from an entirely unique region, the forces impacting their movement would likely be different from those facing northern-PA/other nearby BCCH. If the BCCH closest to us aren't moving, we wouldn't expect hybrids to move more than usual and could assume that an influx of BC-looking/-sounding chickadees likely reflects an influx of pure BCCH, not necessarily hybrids. Understanding that it was possible to have BCCH, potentially in substantial numbers, in this area, I searched for the characteristics that most clearly separate BCCH and CACH. Suthers (2022) is the most recently published reference I could find addressing BC/CA/HYCH identification. They point to patterns in the greater coverts and rectrices as the most reliable for separating BC and CACH from hybrids. The following paragraphs address those points in our bird, as well as other characteristics typically deemed important to identification. - Call: clear audio of slower-cadenced call. Noticeable space between dee notes, which was absent in the CACH call I heard earlier. - Song: would mean nothing on its own, as CACH can learn BCCH songs (and vice versa), but worth noting that I've never heard a chickadee singing a BCCH song in SEPA before. Audio attached. Also, neat that they don't learn calls in the same way that they learn songs... - Highly subjective characteristics that are unreliable without a direct comparison to CACH: this individual appeared large-headed with feathers on the back of the head and neck giving a fluffed appearance. Bill appears relatively heavy - longer culmen would be indicative of BCCH. This bird was not shy. We did nothing to excite it, but it approached us and scolded us seemingly without fear. BCCH are generally thought of as bolder than CACH. - Bib: both sides of the face show black extending down into the bird's chest, and the bib line is clearly broken. Messy bib points to BCCH. - Flanks: dark - almost orange - contrasting heavily with belly and back, not paler as we'd expect in CACH. - Mantle: greenish-brown, not gray as in CACH. - "Cheek": the white on this bird's face is pure and does not slowly transition to gray toward the back of its head as it would in a CACH. - Tertials: Black, not dark gray as in CACH. - Secondaries: bright white edging, good for BCCH. - Rectrices: I obviously didn't get to see the spread tail as you would if you had the bird in your hand, but from what I could tell, multiple pairs of recs had white outer "webs" (Suthers' word, not mine). - Greater coverts: perhaps the most important characteristic, this is the only one that gave me pause. If you look closely, it appears that our bird doesn't have pure white edges to the gr covs. While they are mostly white, the edges may be gray. If there is extensive gray in the gr covs of this bird, that may point to hybridization. Different sources make different claims about the variability of this feature, but I'm inclined to use it as a hybrid indicator. All signs point to this bird being a BCCH. Because we noticed some slight gray edging in its greater coverts, I'm going to send the photos to the experts with the hope of getting their take. Regardless of the outcome, it was neat to finally have a quality encounter with a BC-presenting chickadee down here.  The possibility exists that there was a CACH somewhere in this bird's family tree - even though hybrids are not recognized as being particularly fit. If we are skeptical of hybrids south of the contact zone, then that skepticism would need to extend to CACH-presenting birds, too. We can't be certain that the CACH we see are "pure", but I don't think it would be productive to list chickadees that look and sound like Carolinas as slashes... Because it is outside of its expected range here, any individual suspected of being a BCCH should be investigated as thoroughly as any other "rarity". If all boxes can be checked, listing seems possible. Based on what I've seen, BCCH in this context (region, time of year) can't be reliably identified based on a single characteristic (e.g., white hockey stick; slower call) and we should all gather as much evidence as possible to support our reports of this species.

Messy bib points to BCCH. - Flanks: dark - almost orange - contrasting heavily with belly and back, not paler as we'd expect in CACH. - Mantle: greenish-brown, not gray as in CACH. - "Cheek": the white on this bird's face is pure and does not slowly transition to gray toward the back of its head as it would in a CACH. - Tertials: Black, not dark gray as in CACH. - Secondaries: bright white edging, good for BCCH. - Rectrices: I obviously didn't get to see the spread tail as you would if you had the bird in your hand, but from what I could tell, multiple pairs of recs had white outer "webs" (Suthers' word, not mine). - Greater coverts: perhaps the most important characteristic, this is the only one that gave me pause. If you look closely, it appears that our bird doesn't have pure white edges to the gr covs. While they are mostly white, the edges may be gray. If there is extensive gray in the gr covs of this bird, that may point to hybridization. Different sources make different claims about the variability of this feature, but I'm inclined to use it as a hybrid indicator. All signs point to this bird being a BCCH. Because we noticed some slight gray edging in its greater coverts, I'm going to send the photos to the experts with the hope of getting their take. Regardless of the outcome, it was neat to finally have a quality encounter with a BC-presenting chickadee down here.  The possibility exists that there was a CACH somewhere in this bird's family tree - even though hybrids are not recognized as being particularly fit. If we are skeptical of hybrids south of the contact zone, then that skepticism would need to extend to CACH-presenting birds, too. We can't be certain that the CACH we see are "pure", but I don't think it would be productive to list chickadees that look and sound like Carolinas as slashes... Because it is outside of its expected range here, any individual suspected of being a BCCH should be investigated as thoroughly as any other "rarity". If all boxes can be checked, listing seems possible. Based on what I've seen, BCCH in this context (region, time of year) can't be reliably identified based on a single characteristic (e.g., white hockey stick; slower call) and we should all gather as much evidence as possible to support our reports of this species.

Media notes: The "faint fee-bee" given by male when bringing food to a nesting female. In this recording, the male gives two faint fee-bees from a bush next to the nesting box and then can be heard flying to the box and entering. The female then makes the "squawk" call, usually used signal to young that food is here and they should open up. Squawk first heard at 0:18 and again at 0:30 and 0:31. But no begging sounds in response picked up.  Mic was positioned on the ground angled up to the box. Observed from a distance long enough to see male arrive and depart.
Observation Details: Still struggling to determine if eggs have hatched. I tried with R.'s good mic, placed on the ground and leaned against the shed to point up at the nesting box. A chickadee inside the nesting box repeatedly gave the "squawk" call, "associated with parental provisioning. Apparently produced by parents at the nest, stimulating gaping from the nestlings." But no sound of begging followed.

Media notes: The "faint fee-bee" song, usually given near a nest.
Observation Details: First time hearing the "faint fee-bee", being sung for an extended time in or near the nesting box, where I'd seen a seemingly completed nest last week but wasn't yet in active use. A faint fee-bee set later came from another location near the box; don't know if it's the same bird or not.  From Birds of the World:  "Faint Fee-bee. Resembles Fee-bee song in pattern, but is always much lower in amplitude and often shows higher patterns of variability than loud Fee-bees. Given by both sexes, during the breeding season, usually near the nest. Often given by one adult to its mate, especially when males bring food to females incubating or brooding inside the nest cavity (Ficken et al. 1978); also given by parents to fledged young (Dixon and Stefanski 1970)."

Media notes: The recording was made on a platform feeder containing black oil sunflower seeds.  The phone was on the platform face up, the microphone facing toward the adjacent pine tree, to minimize loss of hopper space.  Sounds heard were made by Black-capped Chickadee (up to 3 individuals), 2 different Red-breasted Nuthatches, a White-breasted Nuthatch.  These sounds include vocalizations, their feet landing on the platform, taking of seed, and wingbeats as they left.  The Chickadees had the loudest wingbeats.  Other birds heard (who did not visit the feeder) include a nearby American Robin, a distant American Crow, and a distant Blue Jay.  This recording has been normalized, but the large dynamic range of the wingbeats tends to minimize the volume of the vocalizations.  At one point near the beginning, a human closes a door.  Insects and light wind are also heard.

It started calling typical Black-capped Chickadee "chik-a-dee" calls, which I was able to record with Merlin. Rare in Mendocino County, this is just the third record (fide Chuck Vaughn). Its tail was in molt, so it's likely been at this site or nearby for a while.

Media notes: Recorded inside a shed with a nesting box mounted outside. Sounds of two chickadees. One is calling from an adjacent bush. The other is working inside the box.  Molded resin makes for some good resonance apparently.  (Includes human construction sounds as well.)
Observation Details: Was in the shed when I heard chickadee contact calls and quiet pecking sounds from the outside of the wall where the nesting box is mounted. Got a recording and then carefully moved back out and away. Saw one chickadee working with the box with the first calling from an adjacent bush. Guess I won't be using the shed much for a bit.  Chickadees do some clear-out work on the nesting site and don't necessarily choose it after doing so. I haven't seen nesting material yet, but among other things they like rabbit fur, plenty of which is available right now with several burrows recently built and fur left outside.   This was the second time I saw chickadees with the box but the first time I could tell something was going on inside.

Observation Details: 15-25 or even 30. biggest group of chickadees so far I’ve found in NY in these past 6 days . — Had just been thinking today how chickadees behave differently here in NY than in Oregon, where on westside we’ve had many flocks of 50 CBCH in just one flock in coast range (not when breeding) and can have dozens of BCCH in each mixed flock in MidValley in winter, and dozens in migration flocks in alders on coast too. Vs so far till this spot, haven’t counted more than 4-7 BCCH in any one group together. — maybe this feeder house is a collecting mix of a few different flocks just coming here for seed in snows? Some flew off downslope while I was here, incl the burry one towards (42.41698, -76.28932). — — 31F snowing  * 1 poss BOCH? — ho 1 burrier chickadee in with BCCH. Boreal would still be a lifer for me so I don’t know what it sounds like really. But I do know BCCH well (the ones in Oregon) and one here sounded burrier . Will check and see if any recordings turned out.  Looking at sonograms, this burry one is different from the others, but most of its chatter seems too similar to BCCH to really be a different species … ? Might just be a weird BCCH?  Looked at Merlin app recordings and a couple from Maine/NY sound similar to my burry one. Merlin doesn’t have any Oregon recordings of BCCH, only NE recordings. This might be a different vocal than in PNW?   Curious why only one chickadee of all of these doing the burry note?  Caleb: Seems interspersed in BCCH gurgle stuff. I think it's in range for BCCH.

Locality: Hammond Hill--Canaan Rd suet feeder house 241 (42.4175,-76.2924) (2023) (1480 ft.)
Observation Details: 15-25 or even 30. biggest group of chickadees so far I’ve found in NY in these past 6 days . — Had just been thinking today how chickadees behave differently here in NY than in Oregon, where on westside we’ve had many flocks of 50 CBCH in just one flock in coast range (not when breeding) and can have dozens of BCCH in each mixed flock in MidValley in winter, and dozens in migration flocks in alders on coast too. Vs so far till this spot, haven’t counted more than 4-7 BCCH in any one group together. — maybe this feeder house is a collecting mix of a few different flocks just coming here for seed in snows? Some flew off downslope while I was here, incl the burry one towards (42.41698, -76.28932). — — 31F snowing  * 1 poss BOCH? — ho 1 burrier chickadee in with BCCH. Boreal would still be a lifer for me so I don’t know what it sounds like really. But I do know BCCH well (the ones in Oregon) and one here sounded burrier . Will check and see if any recordings turned out.  Looking at sonograms, this burry one is different from the others, but most of its chatter seems too similar to BCCH to really be a different species … ? Might just be a weird BCCH?  Looked at Merlin app recordings and a couple from Maine/NY sound similar to my burry one. Merlin doesn’t have any Oregon recordings of BCCH, only NE recordings. This might be a different vocal than in PNW?   Curious why only one chickadee of all of these doing the burry note?  Caleb: Seems interspersed in BCCH gurgle stuff. I think it's in range for BCCH.

Observation Details: A chickadee landed just a couple of feet in front of me to drink while I was sitting on the ground looking at the peeping frogs in a small pond. I saw what looked like a dull hockey stick shape on its wings and the bird called out a couple of "dee" sounds. Listen to the attached audio clip. The rate of the second two slower "dees" is five per second, much slower than the 6+ per second of Carolina chickadees. I think this was a black-capped chickadee.  Photos show bright white hockey stick markings and a somewhat messy bib. It's possible this is a different individual from the one I saw at the pond, but the distance between observations was short. Once I saw the one at the pond, I got up and scanned the trees nearby until I found the group of chickadees and titmice in which the photographed individual was found.  39.93158° N, 75.43489° W just inside deer fence. With other chickadees and titmice and red-breasted nuthatches.

Observation Details: 12:38 PM. Two were calling back and forth from far distance. This is a very interesting interaction because one was calling and the other kept responding. They were also interrupting each other. I could not see either of these chickadees but stood quietly listening to this interaction. The sound would be categorized as "song" versus "call" in this case to be consistent with Macaulay Library audio categorizations. The distinct flute-like, two-note whistle of a high to low pitch is a very distinct sound that we can associate with the Black-capped Chickadee.

Observation Details: I've been trying to pay very close attention to these Black-capped Chickadees. At the very beginning of my walk, I heard and saw 2 BCCH. They responded to my screech owl whistle. I was able to observe them both and see the defining field marks. I walked about 200 yards and a group of chickadees came at me from the front. I believe that it was a distinctly different group from the first that I had seen 200 yards back on the trail. This second group is the one that I recorded. You can hear several BCCH in the recording and one Carolina. There were 3-4 BCCH's in this group. As I've mentioned before, the BCCH respond much more actively to the screech owl whistle than CACH. Later, at the end of the walk, on the opposite end of the park...(nearly a mile to the east) I had 1 more BCCH and verified it with Merlin. Unless the BCCH roam up and down the length of the park so much that I intersect with them several times during one walk, I'd guess that 6 is a conservative number. There are likely more than 6 at Friendship Park on any given day.

Messy bib white hockey sticks etched into secondaries and upperwing coverts buffy flanks slower dee dee dee calls . Point blank looks at the three of them together eye level with a Carolina next to them hanging out a bit north of the recycling center . Another three together about .5 miles away . The second group was also all seen and heard (ten feet tops from me) closer to Belmont plateau in a mixed flock of passerines (TUTI , amro , Wiwr , Cawr , wtsp and those three.) at 39.99164° N, 75.21000° W . Got to look closely at all six birds over the course of a few minutes for each group. Wish I had more time I could have gotten more I bet . Most I’ve seen in Philly on a single checklist so far but a far cry from the county record . The woods looks pretty healthy here relative to other spots in Philadelphia . Made a couple recordings of them while I was looking at that the second group. Pretty familiar with this species at this point in my birding career . I’ve observed them on 18 separate occasions this winter in Philly alone including today and am responsible for 43% of all checklists with bcch recordings in the philly ebird data and 12% of the checklists with bcch photos. Nothing about any of these birds made me think hybrid either. Here’s some past media I’ve collected in Philly of this species : https://media.ebird.org/catalog?taxonCode=bkcchi&userId=USER1912212®ionCode=US-PA-101

Media notes: BCCH recorded as they foraged at a feeder, just after a visit from NSHR. SEET series noted frequently.
Observation Details: Six BCCH were observed and recorded at the sunflower feeders. NSHR was noted a few minutes ago in the feeder area and the BCCH started to just come back to the feeders as I recorded. The "SEET" series of notes (cane shaped notes") were noted frequently in this recording. They may be given in response to danger, and or in courtship. Given the encounter with the NSHR, these may be a vocal response from the BCCH as they return to feed.   A strong "SEET" note was noted at 3 seconds from the first BCCH at the feeder, then the other BCCH followed within 1 minute. The series of "SEET" notes at 20 and 27 seconds were noted from different birds. Water dripping from the trees from the  light rainfall also noted.

Contributed for the female bird song project: femalebirdsong.org. This audio takes place at the nest cavity. The male is already in the nest cavity when female (green band) flies up announcing her presence with multiple feebee calls (typical when food is in hand) but also a pitter call (frequently used when there is a change in duties at the nest). The male refuses to leave the nest and the female flies off. For the associated video see https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PaoQPQh1DEA -- For video and entire series of recordings of this banded female and her male partner at their nest site (including all recordings of song, etc) see: https://www.birdsofyellowstone.com/black-capped-chickadees.

Media notes: windy recording of chickadee call around 4pm

Observation Details: Rare this far south. This yearn a big push of black capped chickadee south has been happening in cuyahoga county and other counties around the great lakes and in the Midwest. The last two days of good north winds have pushed lots of new birds in and  I suspect this bird may have moved in with everything else. I picked up on the Black capped vocalization. Visually white on the greater coverts and secondaries. Olive color in back, more colorful then Carolina. Lower bib line somewhat sloppy and clean.  The bird responded immediately to black capped calls.and flew right in for better photos. Typically from my experience Carolina’s don’t come in to black capped calls. and continue doing what they are doing or actually fly away. I seeked a second opinion from two Ohio ebird reviewers,both agreed  looks good for black cape so I’m submitting this as black capped chickadee.

Saw a larger looking Chickadee fly in the area of the bird feeders along the parking lot by the visitor center but I was somewhat obstructed by parked cars. I tracked it as it crossed the entrance road and was able to get an audio recording and a couple of pictures. The flanks are very buffy and the tail seems long (along with the whiter edges, along the tail, being pretty bold).

Observation Details: I was trying to find good riparian habitat along the Cowichan River and drove to the hatchery, which was gated and signs indicate access is not permitted. I had the windows down and heard a song near the gate that sounded bang on for Black-capped Chickadee. I stopped the car, stepped out, and the bird continued to sing repeatedly, confirming it was not just some off notes in the song of a regular species. At this point, I recorded the song on my phone and proceeded to get views to confirm the identification. I then retrieved my camera and got some decent photos to go with the recording. The first confirmed Vancouver Island record of Black-capped Chickadee from Island View Regional Park - in the area from at least August 20, 2021 to May 1, 2022 - pertained to an individual that was surprisingly shy. The one at the hatchery was very vocal and conspicuous, which leads me to suspect this is not the same individual. Subsequent efforts to relocate the bird have been unsuccessful.

Observation Details: BCCH. Two BCCH were perched facing each other, with about 12 inches between them. The one BCCH was observed to repeatedly, quiver it's wings very quickly and a series of high pitched notes were heard simultaneously. The other bird pecked at the branch it was perched on. This lasted about 20-30 seconds and was repeated once. During the wing quiver, the wings did not go above the body or head as in a wing flap, and there was no antagonistic behavior or chase afterward. This may be a display for mating.

Media notes: There was so much avian activity on this backroad . . . singing, flutterings, peckings, drummings . . . I selected the Sound ID on the Merlin app and holding out my iPhone to catch as much of the orchestration as possible, I was contemplating how incredibly wonderful it all sounded, when I must have become part of the scenery because all of sudden I was spellbound by a Chickadee coming right for me!  I thought, where is he going?? simultaneously holding my breath in wonder when, for a very brief moment, he landed on my pinky! Because of this I did not adjust the amplification.   Also, while I think the whistling bird is a Tufted Titmouse, I am filing this one under Chickadee because the Merlin app did not recognize the Tufted Titmouse.  The drumming is either a Hairy Woodpecker or the Red-bellied Woodpecker---they were both there, although the Pileated Woodpecker was as well, but the drumming is much louder.

This was a group of 5 BCCH. They were gathered in a planting of leafless lilac bushes. Apparently one of them did not belong to the group, or there was some other problem because a dominant bird was chasing a second bird through the shrub. The non-participant birds were doing a lot of chattering. When you hear the intense, louder chattering, that is one bird chasing the other. Not sure who was doing to the talking in that interaction, but it was fast and furious until they landed and then went at it again. I couldn't tell if it was the same bird continually being chased, nor if the chaser was the same bird. there was a lot of chickadee movement in those bushes!

Observation Details: Several groups again, and again a bit difficult to quantify with certainty because of multi-directional movement through the dunes.

Minimum count, but perhaps more than a dozen greater than this. 25 birds was the most that launched at one time, though in that instance they all came back to the dunes. Another departure much later numbered 18 individuals, but only 4 or 5 of those continued to the SW over the bay (getting very high in the process!!). Handfuls attempted here and there, but those two big liftoffs were the main migration events.

Observation Details: BCCH at a feeder in late afternoon. Birds in shrubby hedge near feeder calling in audio 1. In audio 2, the birds at the feeder area suddenly became "still and quiet", just after a few seconds, a numbers of high even pitched call notes were given by a single bird. The BCCH just "froze" motionless as they perched on the feeders, on branches etc. I was not able to get audio of this "warning" call as it was of short duration. The area was very "still", BCCH were not moving their heads or bodies. Then a "Chickadee-dee-dee like call" was given by one bird. It sounded like the first call of audio 2.,  then BCCH's restarted moving, then feeding and then calling again. (later part of Audio 2) . NO hawk or Northern Shrike was noted in the tree's. No cats were noted on the ground.

For the past few days I have been noticing a chickadee that I thought might be Black capped. It appears to be bigger, fluffier and more brightly colored.  After several days of observing I noticed the following details: lots of white on the wings,  bright white cheeks, and an irregular black bib line. I was eventually able to get photos showing most of these details. Observation Details: I have included photos and audio recording. I took the photos immediately after recording the audio to ensure that I was not getting this individual mixed up with the CACH that was also present.  The CACHs were also singing and their "dee dee dee's" were distinctly faster than the call of the BCCH.

TUTI, PSiskin, Jays, robins. I was attempting a recording of a third pass of the group of Siskin, when things got heated. I never was able to ID the aggressor, and I think it may have been located in an adjacent lot. Things fired up about a minute into the recording. This may be an example of cross-species communication or at least how warning signals can travel through a landscape like a wildfire. These birds got hot quickly, and even after they calmed down, no obvious predator seen. But next to the land where I was, multiple Jays seemed to have identified some threat and were “sitting” on it.

Observation Details: FOY property, first heard and then seen in the upper pasture. First alerted by its call, noticeably lower, slower, “skee dee dee”. Bright, contrasting bird with plenty of buff on the flanks, prominent white on wings. The first BCCH seen after a year of CACH is usually very noticeable, so much brighter and crisper. Figured these would show up following the impressive showing of other irruptive species this fall. Fairly poor audio included. Pictures included as well but one view is from below, another is an extremely poor pic but does show some of the white on the wing.

Observation Details: Will add photos and audio recording; heard gruffly "chickadee" call and two-note song. Bright hockey stick on wing, clean white cheek, olive-green back, bright sides, and messy bib. Approached within a couple feet with playback. In brush next to the remote lot. We heard the two-note song and gruffy calls from trees just northeast of the lot. We proceeded to play playback and attracted two birds in. Both of which showed the field marks given above. A third bird was heard singing the BCCH song along with a bird giving CACH type calls from the tree line behind the two close birds.

Known location, part of the southernmost breeding population of Black-capped Chickadees. Slow “fee bee” two-note call (vs. higher, four-note Carolina Chickadee call), slower and lower “chick-a-dee-dee-dee” (vs CACH). Larger head proportionally than CACH with broader and whiter neck/side (nape) area and more scruffy bib outline. Also notice white wing secondary edges (and outside tail feathers to some degree) and uniformly dark tertial feathers, plus buffy sides. Feathers are typically more well worn at this time of year.

Observation Details: Literally swarmed while gardening by these four.  Maybe they were upset that the peanut feeder was empty? - but looked and sounded more like two bickering pairs.  Very agitated; sounded like all four were chirping and calling for several minutes, moving from branch to branch in close proximity to one another. (Two pairs - likely these - have been frequenting the feeder all winter and have always been 'feisty' towards one another, but this behavior today seemed much more intense and aggressive.  Dispute probably territorial in nature but I've never seen any evidence of them breeding in my area before so reluctant to code them yet).

These two most likely were not any of the birds that have fed from my hand in the last week.

Observation Details: (Rare) Reported recently and known consistent county location for black-caps in riparian cottonwoods and alders.  Both seen and heard, recorded. Came in closely with pishing - seen well and from many angles as close as a few feet overhead. Solid cap with no trace of molting white (not a summer molting mountain chickadee - aware of and cautious for the easy misidentification). Lightly buffy sides and white striping on primaries when wings are folded.   Voice a cleaner, sweeter tone with chickadee-dee-dee phrases without squeakiness like a mountain.  Possibly, even likely two birds as I heard a few likely chickadee notes nearby while watching this reported bird.

Media notes: 4 of 4. This cut begins with some gargles, which ordinarily indicate a higher level of aggression than chickadee calls. They may have been stimulated by my squeaking and pishing , or by a short playback of chickadee calls.
Observation Details: Two singing on opposite sides of road (BRP), then four calling, 2 x 2, all on west side of road. The singing was unstimulated by me. I focused on one of the two singers, who was in plain view on a spruce on the roadcut on the Haywood side of the road. I was never able to see his wings. His underparts were unvarying light gray with a hint of buff, just like the bird with white remix-edgings at Haywood Jackson Overlook. The other was on the Jackson side of the road. I don't remember if I played this song back at all. I think not. Chickadeeing erupted on the Jackson side of the road. I never saw any of these birds, but after a while they split and two could be heard in each cluster. They remained in spruces. I played a bit of chickadeeing to them and two did get closer. I interpret the chickadee-calling as a boundary dispute between two pairs. Some gargles can be heard in the recordings.

About as good as it’s going to get for an uncollected BCCH in NC. Singing hey sweetie from an exposed perch on the top of a spruce, in two different keys, showing lots of white in the wings. Later giving chickadee calls with very slow Dee notes. The sighting was along the paved trail.

Media notes: This cut came first in time.
Observation Details: About as good as it’s going to get for an uncollected BCCH in NC. Singing hey sweetie from an exposed perch on the top of a spruce, in two different keys, showing lots of white in the wings. Later giving chickadee calls with very slow Dee notes. The sighting was along the paved trail.

Media notes: Notice the bird has shifted frequencies here, compared to ML165386961, which was recorded first. Unfortunately the switch was made while I had recorder off to glass the bird. This is a well-known BCCH practice, giving them some repertoire variety, as unlike CACH, they sing only one song-type ("Hey-Sweetie") in most of the ir range. Back when this was discovered, Bernie Lohr, then at Duke, tried but did not find a similar practice in CACH.

Observation Details: I heard these chickadees from my cabin.  I have several bird feeders with sunflower seed and a couple bags of suet.  They've been coming around regularly all winter.  Yesterday was the first day I heard a few tentative "spring" songs, as I call them.  Today they were just going at it.  There's at least 2 individuals singing the spring song and others are in the neighborhood vocalizing in other ways from time to time.  I don't think I've ever heard them singing so heartily this early in the year.  Usually mid February is about normal.  This was the second warm day after a couple weeks of cold (near or below 0F)  It was about 20F, mostly sunny, calm.  I recorded a few brief general scenes.  The birds sang for almost an hour.  The birds sang from nearby my cabin from spruces (mostly dead from beetle kill) and birches.

Observation Details: I know this is well south of its range in Ohio. The two-note call caught my attention initially, which I tried to record (attached) among the raucous Blue Jays. After I got the recordings, it flew higher into the trees. At that point, I got a couple distant, heavily-cropped photos. In addition to its song, plumage markers supporting BCCH include: it appeared to have a “messier,” less-defined bib line; the brighter white coloring seemed to extend beyond the face back to the nape area where Carolinas are duller and grayer; and the tail appears relatively long. Couldn’t get a good look at the secondaries, as they were never visible in its positioning. I know BCCH and CACH hybridize and can even learn one another’s songs, but the song and plumage markers together seem to indicate this was a BCCH. Given that the remnants of Hurricane Florence just blew through from the SE, would it be possible that this could be a WV/Appalachian bird that was blown in as opposed to a northern Ohio bird?

Found singing "see-boo" song at 711am & immediately began recording as I approached it. Eventually seen well with nice extensive white in coverts & primary edgings & this individual showing noticeably buff on the flanks. And seen again 2 hrs later in same exact location, in company of Carolinas for comparison. GRCA, AMRO, WAVI heard in background, along with my footsteps crunchin in gravel. Appears to be first documented eBird record for the month of Sept for Phila. Only two previous reports appear likely to refer to Carolina Chickadee.

A pair and four noisy fledglings, each parent seemingly responsible for feeding two. Roving from our yard to the properties across the street. The cavity in our birch that was constructed starting in March was abandoned earlier in the season, most likely due to the constant attention of bothersome House Sparrows which ended up nesting in the gutter less than two meters away. The male continued to sing on our property, so I suspected they had relocated to a backup location close by. While it would've been nice to watch the feedings and fledging from my kitchen window, I'm delighted to see that our pair managed to nest successfully this year despite the obstacles

Observation Details: Minimum exact count, including at least 3 singing "fe-be." Heard high-pitched calls from chickadees near the E property boundary along the stream at 0927, at which point I noticed the falcon was gone from its perch. Between this time and ca 0945, observed lots of activity from at least 7 chickadees in this area, including quite a bit of social behavior, with 2 to 5 birds flying around and flitting from perch to perch in a close area. This seemed to include some chases and displacement of birds from perches. For a few minutes around 0948 the chickadees in this area were very quiet, while I heard magpies calling a lot from downstream. Wondered if a predator was around but if so did not find it. At 0952, observed an apparent aggressive chase between 2 individuals in the E part of reach 4, with one close behind the other for ca 10 m. Immediate after, I heard high, soft "se-se-se" calls (different from the high se-se-se predator alarm calls) dropping into a gargle from one of the two - not sure which one. Heard at least 2 of the chickadee group as far upstream as the red culvert. Later, heard a BCCH singing between the diversion and the rocky draw, then observed it foraging near me and got photos and a recording. Wondered if it was perhaps another in addition to the previous 7, but later sightings suggested to me that I could not be sure of that. The snow depth where I observed it along the N bank of the creek was about 57 cm; this chickadee appeared to be solitary, as with the sightings on 7 Mar. Ca 1300, I heard and saw 1 BCCH in the vicinity of the cross-fence, singing and making "de-de-de" calls, only after I had passed by it. Later, I heard at least 2 chickadees near the turnaround, 1 singing. On the return I encountered 2 BCCH at the diversion.

Locality: Sevenmile Creek--Prickly Pear Land Trust (restricted access)
Observation Details: Heard "fee-bee" song early in the survey from downstream, beyond the property, along the stream. Later, near the reach 3/4 boundary, I heard one making soft calls, some multi-syllabic. Soon I saw three chickadees in the vicinity and recorded gargle calls and other vocalizations. They appeared to be gleaning from branches of shrubs along the creek.  Several times later in the survey, I encountered at least 2 BCCH farther upstream and strongly suspected they were some of the same individuals. I noted them: at 1113, ca 50 m below the diversion; at 1124, flying to ca 50-100 m above the diversion; at 1149, foraging along the stream shortly above the rocky draw; and at 1250, in shrubs shortly downstream of the cross-fence.
A more thorough attempt was made on this trip to the park to study the chickadees here. In both looks and voice, the vast majority of birds continue to appear to be BCCH.
6 were seen in long grasses by Grenadier Pond, another 2 were in the forest by Grenadier pond.

Media notes: Black-capped Chickadee with Spotted Towhee and American Crow also in the recording
